front: Work: this six-book poetic work opens with the 'Song of the Reed Flute,' instructing the reader to 'listen to the story told by the reed / of being separated'
Work: in this work, Moses erroneously rebukes a shepherd for offering to comb God's hair and pick his lice
Work: this work contains a version of the blind men and the elephant parable, ending with the metaphor of distinguishing foam from the sea
Work: this work contains the biography of Ibrahim ibn Adham, who gave up the crown of Bactria to become an ascetic
Work: this work opens by describing itself as the 'explainer' of the Quran
Work: scholars identify seven 'voices' in this work, including the 'Hiatus' voice where the author says his true intentions are inexpressible
Work: this collection of ghazals is dedicated to Rumi's teacher Shams of Tabriz, inspired by Shams's disappearance
Work: a poem in this collection describes a field 'out beyond ideas of rightdoing and wrongdoing'; the original references 'religion' and 'infidelity'
Work: the poem 'Like This' asks the 'dissolver of sugar' to 'dissolve me, if this is the time'
Work: 'The Guest House' states 'This being human is a guest house / Every morning a new arrival'; Coleman Barks read it on Coldplay's 'Kaleidoscope'
Author: Coleman Barks's free-verse 'translations' of this poet are criticized for erasing Islamic references; Barks neither reads nor writes Persian
Author: Robert Bly introduced Coleman Barks to this poet by giving him A.J. Arberry's literal translation, saying the poems needed to be 'released from their cages'
Author: a 2017 New Yorker article discussed 'The Erasure of Islam from the Poetry of' this poet; Omid Safi called it 'spiritual colonialism'
Author: this poet was taught by Shams of Tabriz in seclusion for 40 days; Shams later disappeared, inspiring a divan
Author: in one legend, this poet's teacher threw his books in a pool; they remained dry, and the teacher said 'Something you do not know'
Author: this poet's tomb in Konya is a shrine for the Mevlevi order, named after him (from his title Mawlana)
Author: Elif Shafak's novel The Forty Rules of Love was inspired by this poet and his advisor; features homemaker Ella Rosenberg
Author: this poet befriended a goldsmith (Salah al-Din Zarkub) to replace Shams as his muse after Shams disappeared
Work: Fihi Ma Fihi — this author wrote a collection of 72 dialogues whose title translates as 'It is what it is'
